content:
First note that any “block’’ of $k$ consecutive 1’s represents the integer 
\[
\underbrace{11\cdots1}_{k} \;=\;\frac{10^k-1}9\;=\;(10^k-1)\,/\,9.
\]
If in total we break our string into blocks of lengths 
$$\underbrace{1,1,\dots,1}_{x\text{ times}},\;
\underbrace{2,2,\dots,2}_{y\text{ times}},\;
\underbrace{3,3,\dots,3}_{z\text{ times}},$$
then the sum of the blocks is
\[
x\cdot\frac{10^1-1}9\;+\;y\cdot\frac{10^2-1}9\;+\;z\cdot\frac{10^3-1}9
\;=\;\frac{9x+99y+999z}9
\]
and we want this to equal $1000$.  Hence
\[
9x+99y+999z \;=\;9000
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
x+11y+111z \;=\;1000.
\]
The total length of the string is 
\[
n \;=\; x+2y+3z.
\]
From
\[
x \;=\;1000-11y-111z
\quad(\ge0),
\]
one checks easily that only block‐sizes $1,2,3$ occur, and that
\[
n \;=\;(1000-11y-111z)\;+\;2y\;+\;3z
\;=\;1000 \;-\;9\,(y+12z).
\]
Set 
\[
t=y+12z,
\quad
0\le t\le\bigl\lfloor1000/9\bigr\rfloor=111,
\]
and one finds by a small check that all $t=0,1,2,\dots,111$ except
\[
t=107,109,110,111
\]
actually occur.  Hence there are 
\[
112\;\text{values of }t\;-\;4
\;=\;108
\]
possible values of~$n$.  Therefore 
\[
a \;=\;108,
\]
and the sum of the digits of $a$ is
\[
1+0+8 \;=\;9.
\]
\boxed{9}

\[
\underbrace{11\cdots1}_{k} \;=\;\frac{10^k-1}9\;=\;(10^k-1)\,/\,9.

\underbrace{3,3,\dots,3}_{z\text{ times}},$$
then the sum of the blocks is
\[
x\cdot\frac{10^1-1}9\;+\;y\cdot\frac{10^2-1}9\;+\;z\cdot\frac{10^3-1}9

x \;=\;1000-11y-111z
\quad(\ge0),

\;=\;1000 \;-\;9\,(y+12z).
\]

\[
t=y+12z,
\quad
0\le t\le\bigl\lfloor1000/9\bigr\rfloor=111,
\]
and one finds by a small check that all $t=0,1,2,\dots,111$ except
\[

\]
actually occur.  Hence there are 
\[

\;=\;108
\]
possible values of~$n$.  Therefore 
\[
a \;=\;108,
\]
and the sum of the digits of $a$ is
\[
1+0+8 \;=\;9.
\]
\boxed{9}
